institution: University of Texas Southwestern Medical Center

ai_strategy_lead.name: Steve Jiang, Ph.D.
ai_strategy_lead.title: Vice Chair for Digital Health and AI, Department of Radiation Oncology; Director, Medical Artificial Intelligence and Automation (MAIA) Lab; Barbara Crittenden Professor in Cancer Research

documents.title: Artificial Intelligence with Meaning (AIM) — UT Southwestern AI overview
Using AI at UT Southwestern — employee campus update on AI platform rollout

ai_strategy.summary: UT Southwestern frames its AI program as 'AI with Meaning' (AIM), organized around four domains — clinical care, research, education, and administration — with responsible-use, HIPAA, and FERPA compliance as explicit guardrails. Implementation is led by a four-executive oversight group (CIO Russell Poole, Chief Compliance & Audit Officer Natalie A. Ramello, General Counsel Erin M. Sine, and Chief Information Security Officer Tony Lakin). Enterprise rollout was rescheduled to December 1, 2025 to align integration, governance, subscription management, training, and a central AI Resource Website. UTSW also participates in the systemwide UT-REAL-Health-AI initiative ($25M) and the Microsoft-anchored Trustworthy & Responsible AI Network (TRAIN).

key_provisions: Users must use an institutionally-approved AI tool for work-related tasks; consumer/general ChatGPT is not permitted for PHI or protected data
HIPAA, FERPA, and UT System privacy compliance required for all AI workflows
Centralized subscription allocation and management across the enterprise
Mandatory 'Principles of Responsible and Ethical Conduct when using Generative AI' training via Percipio
Four-executive oversight structure (CIO, CISO, General Counsel, Chief Compliance & Audit Officer) governs enterprise AI deployment
Approved-tool catalog with explicit pricing tiers, audiences, and integration status
ai_policy.summary: UTSW operates a comprehensive, centrally governed AI policy anchored by the December 1, 2025 enterprise platform launch. Guidance specifies approved vendors (Microsoft 365 Copilot, Microsoft Teams Premium, OpenAI ChatGPT, Cloudforce nebulaONE on UTSW Azure, Qualified Health, and others in pilot), data-handling rules, and mandatory responsible-use training. The stance is permissive toward approved tools but prohibitive toward consumer AI use with PHI or sensitive data.

bodies.scope: Enterprise-wide AI deployment, integration, governance, and compliance for the Dec 2025 platform rollout

bodies.name: AI in Healthcare Oversight Committee / AI Taskforce
bodies.scope: Reviews AI use cases in clinical and operational contexts; coordinates with IRB and executive leadership

bodies.name: Ethics in AI Working Group (U-HACK MED)
bodies.scope: Facilitates programmatic inclusion of AI ethics in graduate/medical education; hosts educational events. Led by Darlene King, M.D. and Prapti Mody, M.D. (2025)

bodies.name: Informatics Coordinating Office (ICO)
bodies.scope: Enterprise informatics training and education, including AI-adjacent programs

bodies.name: UT System Data and AI Ecosystem workgroups
bodies.scope: Five systemwide workgroups covering technology platforms, data acquisition, governance, non-Microsoft tools, and project enablement; UTSW leaders include DuWayne Willett (CMIO), Brian Evans (Chief Enterprise Architect), Tammye Garrett (Chief Data Officer)

governance_structure.summary: UTSW operates a multi-layer governance model: an executive four-person oversight group for enterprise AI rollout, a clinical AI oversight committee for healthcare use cases, an ethics working group embedded in bioinformatics education, and system-level participation in the UT Health Intelligence Platform and UT-REAL-Health-AI governance. A single dedicated Chief AI Officer has not been named.

initiatives.title: Enterprise AI Platform Launch (AIM)
initiatives.description: UT Southwestern rescheduled the introduction of its enterprise AI platform to December 1, 2025 after finalizing integrations, institutional standards, guidance documents, training resources, and subscription allocation processes. Launch includes AI Town Halls, targeted training for research/clinical/administrative users, and a new AI Resource Website.
initiatives.significance: Shows a major academic medical center operationalizing enterprise AI with an explicit executive oversight structure rather than shadow-IT adoption.

investment.description: Enterprise AI program with centrally managed subscriptions and a tool catalog spanning Microsoft 365 Copilot, Teams Premium, OpenAI ChatGPT, Cloudforce nebulaONE, Qualified Health, Perplexity, and planned Microsoft Foundry, Google Gemini/Vertex AI, and Anthropic Claude Code deployments.

collaborations.description: Multi-vendor AI tooling strategy with centralized governance

initiatives.title: Trustworthy & Responsible AI Network (TRAIN) founding member
initiatives.description: In March 2024, UT Southwestern joined Microsoft and more than a dozen leading academic medical centers (Cleveland Clinic, Duke, Johns Hopkins, Mass General Brigham, Mount Sinai, Northwestern, Vanderbilt, and others) to launch TRAIN — a consortium setting shared standards for safe, fair, and reliable AI in healthcare, a secure AI tool registration portal, and outcome-measurement tools.
initiatives.significance: Anchors UTSW in a national governance consortium shaping responsible AI standards for hospital systems.

investment.funding_source: Consortium membership; Microsoft as technology-enabling partner
investment.description: Consortium contribution; financial terms not publicly disclosed

partners: University of Wisconsin School of Medicine and Public Health

tools_and_vendors.name: TRAIN secure registration portal
tools_and_vendors.role: AI tool registration and outcome measurement

initiatives.policy_implications: Aligns UTSW AI governance to an emerging sector standard for healthcare AI safety and quality.

initiatives.title: UT-REAL-Health-AI systemwide initiative
initiatives.description: On June 24, 2025, the UT System announced $25 million ($15M from the Texas Legislature, $10M from the Board of Regents) to launch the UT Research, Engineering, and Application Laboratory for Healthcare AI. It establishes a statewide AI model inventory, a HIPAA-compliant generative AI testing platform, secure data-sharing labs, cloud/computing resources, and recruits clinicians, developers, and AI experts. UTSW is one of the participating UT health institutions.
initiatives.significance: Positions the UT System (and UTSW) as a nationally visible, state-funded testbed for responsible healthcare AI.

investment.funding_source: Texas Legislature ($15M) and UT System Board of Regents ($10M)
investment.description: Systemwide AI laboratory for healthcare, coordinated through UT Health Intelligence Platform (UT-HIP)

collaborations.description: UT Health Intelligence Platform member institutions
State appropriation and regental allocation funding the laboratory

initiatives.policy_implications: Institutionalizes shared AI model inventory, testing, and governance across Texas's largest public healthcare-research footprint.

initiatives.title: AI-driven medical data extraction pipeline (kidney cancer)
initiatives.description: A multidisciplinary UTSW team from the Lyda Hill Department of Bioinformatics, Pathology, and Urology built an LLM-based pipeline that extracts structured data from free-text pathology reports. Validated on 2,200+ kidney cancer cases and 3,500+ additional records, achieving 99% accuracy on tumor type and 97% on metastasis detection. Published in npj Digital Medicine (July 28, 2025).
initiatives.significance: Demonstrates how academic medical centers can convert unstructured clinical text into analysis-ready datasets, accelerating cancer research and quality measurement.

investment.funding_source: National Cancer Institute Kidney Cancer SPORE (P50 CA196516); Brock Fund for Medical Science Chair in Pathology
investment.description: NCI SPORE and endowed chair funding supporting the research team

collaborations.description: NCI SPORE funding the underlying cancer research program

tools_and_vendors.name: Custom LLM pipeline
tools_and_vendors.role: Extracts and standardizes structured data from pathology reports

initiatives.title: UT System AI Symposium in Health Care (inaugural)
initiatives.description: UT Southwestern hosted the inaugural UT System AI Symposium in Health Care on May 30-31, 2024, co-chaired by Yang Xie (UTSW), Ying Ding (UT Austin), and Hongfang Liu (UTHealth Houston), with Nigam Shah (Stanford) and Peter Stone (UT Austin) as keynote speakers. Eight UT System entities participated across education, biomedical research, and medical-care tracks.
initiatives.significance: Establishes UTSW as a convening center for UT System healthcare AI activity and cross-institution collaboration.

collaborations.description: UT System-wide convening with external keynotes

initiatives.title: Program in Applied Artificial Intelligence in Clinical Imaging (PAAICI)
initiatives.description: Housed in the Department of Radiology, PAAICI drives basic discovery, translation, and clinical deployment of AI/ML in imaging. Deployed tools include algorithms that detect and flag possible intracranial hemorrhage on head CT, cervical spine fractures, and pulmonary embolism, and prioritize radiologist review — with documented improvements in turnaround time.
initiatives.significance: Example of a research program successfully translating AI into routine clinical radiology workflows.

investment.description: Imaging AI research and clinical translation

tools_and_vendors.name: ICH detection algorithm
tools_and_vendors.role: Flags possible intracranial hemorrhage on head CT

tools_and_vendors.name: C-spine fracture detection
tools_and_vendors.role: Computer-aided detection in cervical spine imaging

initiatives.policy_implications: Demonstrates clinical AI operating under FDA/regulatory and UTSW radiology governance.

initiatives.title: Medical Artificial Intelligence and Automation (MAIA) Lab
initiatives.description: The MAIA Lab, led by Steve Jiang, Ph.D. in the Department of Radiation Oncology, develops AI for treatment planning, medical imaging, outcome prediction, and related clinical applications. The lab supports open-source databases, specialized personnel, and academic-industrial collaborations.
initiatives.significance: One of UTSW's flagship AI-in-medicine labs with a decade-plus track record in radiation oncology automation.

investment.description: Laboratory operations supported by institutional funds, grants, and donations

collaborations.description: Collaborations with academic and industrial partners (unnamed on public page)

initiatives.title: BioHPC — academic AI computing infrastructure
initiatives.description: UTSW operates BioHPC, centered on the Nucleus compute cluster (~800 nodes, 28,000+ CPU cores, 100 GPU nodes including NVIDIA P4/P40/P100/V100/H100 cards, 60 large-memory GPU nodes), backed by Lysosome (21 PB high-performance storage, DDN SFA400NVX2) and Lamella cloud storage gateway. 30+ PB of high-performance storage and ~100 PB cold storage support AI and computational biomedicine.
initiatives.significance: One of Texas's largest academic HPC footprints dedicated to biomedical AI and ML workloads.

investment.description: Dedicated HPC/GPU infrastructure for AI, bioinformatics, and imaging

tools_and_vendors.name: Nucleus
tools_and_vendors.role: Main HPC cluster with CPU and GPU nodes

initiatives.title: U-HACK MED and Ethics in AI working group
initiatives.description: U-HACK MED is a biomedical hackathon program in the Lyda Hill Department of Bioinformatics launched in 2018. Its Ethics in AI working group facilitates inclusion of AI ethics in graduate and medical education and hosts periodic events; led by Darlene King, M.D. and Prapti Mody, M.D. as of 2025. A February 2026 two-day nanocourse on 'Ethics in AI — Scientific Writing' covers AI detection and ethical use in research writing.
initiatives.significance: Integrates AI ethics and hackathon-style experiential learning into medical and graduate education.

collaborations.description: Open to UTSW and broader biomedical community

initiatives.policy_implications: Provides the academic scaffolding for institutional AI ethics discourse.

initiatives.title: AI-enhanced medical education (Jamieson Lab / OSCE grading)
initiatives.description: The Jamieson Lab (Andrew Jamieson, Ph.D., Lyda Hill Department of Bioinformatics) builds multimodal AI models that integrate video, audio, and text to analyze communication and diagnostic reasoning in medical students' Objective Structured Clinical Examinations (OSCEs). The system streamlines reviewing and grading of student clinical work.
initiatives.significance: Indicates AI moving from research topic to active educational-assessment infrastructure at an R1 medical school.

collaborations.description: Internal UTSW collaborations across medical education

initiatives.title: Xie Lab — AI and imaging analysis for lung cancer
initiatives.description: The Xie Lab, led by Yang Xie, Ph.D. (Associate Dean for Data Sciences), combines AI with imaging analysis to develop personalized treatment strategies for non-small cell lung cancer within the Quantitative Biomedical Research Center.
initiatives.significance: Illustrative of translational AI-in-oncology research at UTSW and the QBRC, feeding the broader AIM clinical-care portfolio.

initiatives.title: Clinical Informatics Fellowship and Health Informatics M.S.
initiatives.description: UTSW runs a two-year ACGME-accredited Clinical Informatics Fellowship and a two-year Master of Science in Health Informatics through the School of Health Professions. Curriculum includes machine learning, predictive analytics, and evaluation/integration of AI-driven solutions into clinical workflows.
initiatives.significance: Workforce pipeline producing physicians and informaticians prepared for AI-enabled clinical practice.

partners: Accreditation Council for Graduate Medical Education (ACGME)

initiatives.title: Secure HIPAA-compliant ChatGPT via OpenAI collaboration
initiatives.description: UT Southwestern has partnered with OpenAI (alongside systemwide UTHealth Houston activity) to facilitate secure, HIPAA-compliant ChatGPT access for clinicians, researchers, and administrators, with generic consumer ChatGPT prohibited for PHI. Access is surfaced through the UTSW approved-tools catalog.
initiatives.significance: Moves enterprise-grade generative AI into clinical and research workflows without violating privacy law.

collaborations.description: HIPAA-compliant ChatGPT delivery via enterprise agreements and UTSW Azure tenant (nebulaONE)

computing_infrastructure.summary: BioHPC operates the Nucleus cluster (~800 nodes, 28,000+ CPU cores, 100 GPU nodes including NVIDIA P4/P40/P100/V100/H100 cards, 60 large-memory GPU nodes), Lysosome storage (21 PB raw, DDN SFA400NVX2), and Lamella cloud-storage gateway. Total 30+ PB high-performance and ~100 PB cold storage, accessible via web browser. Described as one of Texas's largest academic HPC environments.

regulatory_context.summary: Texas passed HB 149 (Texas Responsible AI Governance framework) creating a statewide Artificial Intelligence Council and baseline prohibitions on social-scoring and harmful AI design; effective January 1, 2026. At the system level, UT operates under Board of Regents' Rules and Regulations and UTS policies, and the UT-REAL-Health-AI/UT-HIP initiative provides a shared AI governance framework across health institutions including UTSW. Texas SB 17 (anti-DEI) also constrains equity-adjacent programming at public universities.

narrative_summary: UT Southwestern Medical Center's AI posture is defined by disciplined enterprise governance layered on a deep biomedical research base. The institution frames its strategy as 'AI with Meaning' (AIM) across four domains — clinical care, research, education, and administration — with a December 1, 2025 enterprise platform launch overseen by a four-executive council (CIO Russell Poole, CISO Tony Lakin, General Counsel Erin M. Sine, Chief Compliance & Audit Officer Natalie A. Ramello). A centrally managed approved-tool catalog spans Microsoft 365 Copilot, Teams Premium, ChatGPT, Cloudforce nebulaONE (GPT in UTSW Azure), Qualified Health, and Perplexity, with pilots of Microsoft Foundry, Google Gemini/Vertex AI, and Anthropic Claude Code staged through 2026. Research activity is anchored by the Lyda Hill Department of Bioinformatics (2015), the MAIA Lab (radiation oncology AI under Steve Jiang), the Program in Applied AI in Clinical Imaging (PAAICI) in Radiology, the QBRC's Xie Lab, and the Jamieson Lab, supported by BioHPC's ~800-node Nucleus cluster (100 GPU nodes, H100s among others) and 30+ PB storage. Educational pathways include PhD programs in Computational and Systems Biology, an M.S. in Health Informatics, and an ACGME Clinical Informatics Fellowship; AI ethics content is delivered through U-HACK MED nanocourses and a working group led by Darlene King and Prapti Mody. UTSW is embedded in national and state consortia: the Microsoft-anchored Trustworthy & Responsible AI Network (TRAIN, March 2024, 17 members), the UT System's Data and AI Ecosystem, and the $25M UT-REAL-Health-AI initiative funded in June 2025 by the Texas Legislature and Board of Regents. Clinical AI is operational in radiology (ICH, C-spine fracture, PE detection), and published research includes a 2025 LLM pipeline that extracts structured data from pathology reports at 99% tumor-type accuracy. No dedicated Chief AI Officer is yet named; AI leadership is distributed across the CIO, CMIO (DuWayne Willett), Chief Data Officer (Tammye Garrett), Chief Enterprise Architect (Brian Evans), and research leaders. The posture is comprehensive and compliance-forward — approved tools for approved uses, with HIPAA and FERPA governing data handling.

gaps_acknowledged: Specific disclosed dollar amounts for UTSW's share of the UT-REAL-Health-AI $25M and for the enterprise AI platform rollout are not publicly itemized.
No dedicated Chief AI Officer has been named; governance is distributed across CIO/CISO/Compliance/General Counsel and CMIO/Chief Data Officer/Chief Enterprise Architect.

Specific FDA-clearance vendors deployed in UTSW Radiology (Aidoc, Viz.ai, Cleerly, etc.) were not confirmed in public sources; only internally-developed algorithms (ICH, C-spine, PE) were directly attributed.
UTSW has no published syllabus-level AI policy equivalent to undergraduate-focused institutions (medical/graduate-only campus).
User counts for enterprise AI platform deployments are not disclosed.
No specific controversies or litigation related to AI were located.

strategic_archetype.rationale: Primary archetype selected from visible public signals: platform first.
